lecture_context.lecture_title: Lecture 16 - Efficient Training and Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning

unit.name: Multi-GPU basics: DDP and ZeRO stage 1
unit.description: Introduces memory accounting on multi-GPU training, DDP synchronization, and optimizer-state sharding in ZeRO stage 1.
unit.summary: The lecture switches to the multi-GPU setting by accounting for what lives in GPU VRAM: model parameters, gradients, and optimizer state, with Adam requiring momentum and variance buffers in FP32 in addition to mixed-precision model weights [ts=852s][ts=913s]. In distributed data parallel training, the dataset is split across GPUs, each replica computes different activations and gradients on its shard, and synchronization communicates those gradients so every optimizer can apply the same update [ts=923s][ts=1034s]. The all-reduce primitive is introduced as the operation that merges gradients and redistributes the combined result to every worker, with communication cost tied to FP16 gradient size [ts=985s][ts=1018s]. The instructor then highlights DDP's weak memory scaling because each GPU still stores the full model, the full gradient, FP32 master weights, and Adam state, summarized as 2 bytes for parameters, 2 for gradients, and 12 extra bytes per parameter for optimizer-related state [ts=1065s][ts=1092s]. ZeRO stage 1 improves this by sharding optimizer state so each worker updates only the parameters corresponding to its shard after a reduce-scatter, then synchronizes updated parameters with an all-gather [ts=1165s][ts=1298s].

key_points.text: In mixed-precision multi-GPU training, Adam still needs FP32 optimizer buffers for momentum and variance.
DDP splits data across workers, so each model replica gets different activations and gradients before synchronization.
All-reduce merges gradients across GPUs and redistributes the result to all workers.
DDP memory scales poorly because every GPU keeps the full model, full gradients, FP32 master weights, and optimizer state.
ZeRO stage 1 shards optimizer state so each worker updates only the parameters associated with its shard.
Reduce-scatter followed by all-gather lets ZeRO stage 1 preserve synchronization while cutting optimizer-state memory.

In distributed data parallel training, the dataset is split across GPUs, each replica computes different activations and gradients on its shard, and synchronization communicates those gradients so every optimizer can apply the same update [ts=923s][ts=1034s]. The all-reduce primitive is introduced as the operation that merges gradients and redistributes the combined result to every worker, with communication cost tied to FP16 gradient size [ts=985s][ts=1018s]. The instructor then highlights DDP's weak memory scaling because each GPU still stores the full model, the full gradient, FP32 master weights, and Adam state, summarized as 2 bytes for parameters, 2 for gradients, and 12 extra bytes per parameter for optimizer-related state [ts=1065s][ts=1092s]. ZeRO stage 1 improves this by sharding optimizer state so each worker updates only the parameters corresponding to its shard after a reduce-scatter, then synchronizes updated parameters with an all-gather [ts=1165s][ts=1298s].

kp_catalog.name: Distributed Data Parallel (DDP)
kp_catalog.description: Multi-GPU training setup where each worker holds a full replica, processes a data shard, and synchronizes gradients across workers.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because distributed Data Parallel (DDP) formalizes how multi-GPU training setup where each worker holds a full replica, processes a data shard, and synchronizes gradients across workers, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.

kp_catalog.name: Collective communication primitives
kp_catalog.description: All-reduce, reduce-scatter, all-gather, and reduce as the core synchronization operations behind DDP, ZeRO, and FSDP.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because collective communication primitives clarifies all-reduce, reduce-scatter, all-gather, and reduce as the core synchronization operations behind DDP, ZeRO, and FSDP, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: ZeRO optimizer and gradient sharding
kp_catalog.description: ZeRO stages 1 and 2 reduce memory by sharding optimizer state and then gradients across workers while preserving synchronized updates.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because zeRO optimizer and gradient sharding formalizes how zeRO stages 1 and 2 reduce memory by sharding optimizer state and then gradients across workers while preserving synchronized updates, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.
